headline: 2 bridge contracts catalogued across Arbitrum mainnet and testnet; validator-governed 2/3-quorum authorization model with dispute-period security mechanism

findings.text: Arbitrum block explorer name-tag search: 1 address found tagged 'Hyperliquid: Deposit Bridge 2' at 0x2df1c51e09aecf9cacb7bc98cb1742757f163df7

Official Bridge2 documentation at https://hyperliquid.gitbook.io/hyperliquid-docs/for-developers/api/bridge2 lists mainnet bridge address 0x2df1c51e09aecf9cacb7bc98cb1742757f163df7 and testnet address 0x08cfc1B6b2dCF36A1480b99353A354AA8AC56f89; no canonical deployed_contracts_doc page found beyond individual docs
Zellic audit (November 2023) scoped Bridge2.sol and Signature.sol contracts; commits 204f017e, 2ad53ac9, 9189a7dd on Arbitrum; no vulnerabilities discovered

GitHub repository https://github.com/hyperliquid-dex/contracts contains master branch with Bridge2.sol and Signature.sol source files; no hardhat-deploy or Foundry broadcast artifacts discovered; manual source inspection only

Multi-chain enumeration: Arbitrum mainnet (1 address); Arbitrum testnet/Sepolia (1 address); Hyperliquid L1 (no bridge contract deployed on L1, only on Arbitrum - bridge operates in reverse direction from L1 to Arbitrum)
No factory or child contract enumeration required; Bridge2.sol is a singleton contract, not a factory

Role taxonomy applied: Bridge2 is core bridge contract; proxy_admin inferred from Medium analysis describing AdminUpgradeabilityProxy pattern; validators and lockers identified as governance/admin roles

All addresses trace to fetched URLs: Arbitrum mainnet from GitBook docs and Arbiscan/block explorer search; testnet from GitBook Bridge2 API docs; deployer from codeslaw.app (https://www.codeslaw.app)

rationale.verdict: Walked GitBook docs, GitHub source, Zellic audit PDF, and block explorer pages. Identified 2 contract deployments (1 mainnet, 1 testnet) across Arbitrum chains. No corresponding contracts found on Hyperliquid L1 chain itself (bridge is unidirectional Arbitrum→Hyperliquid). Documented governance roles (hot/cold validators, lockers, finalizers) from audit findings. Source repository and audit links verified.

evidence.shows: Audit links page listing Zellic audits (November 2023 patch review and August 2023 initial audit); first report mentions bridge contract security assessment

Zellic Smart Contract Patch Review (Nov 27, 2023) scoping Bridge2.sol and Signature.sol on Arbitrum; commits 204f017e52954d9c6814f1427bb66aa78bc6c07c, 2ad53ac96bd6103b4194ace23041fc20fedd96a9, 9189a7ddeb410e835548c1190414949f04311bfa; zero vulnerabilities discovered

Bridge2 documentation providing mainnet address 0x2df1c51e09aecf9cacb7bc98cb1742757f163df7 (Arbitrum) and testnet address 0x08cfc1B6b2dCF36A1480b99353A354AA8AC56f89 (Arbitrum Sepolia); deposit and withdrawal mechanics

Bridge documentation describing validator governance: 2/3 cold wallet validator signatures required to unlock bridge; dispute period mechanism; locker-based locking; finalizer role for executing withdrawals
Public repository containing Bridge2.sol and Signature.sol source contracts; 25 commits; no deployment artifacts found

Bridge2.sol source code; comments indicate validator set management with hot/cold wallet distinction; epoch-based updates; dispute period for withdrawals and validator changes; locker and finalizer roles

Arbitrum contract details showing deployer 0x1D4c01E15A637cB3cbaF86fFbb02E5A260D01fbc; ABI shows constructor taking hotAddresses, coldAddresses, powers, usdcAddress, dispute period, block duration, and locker threshold parameters; methods include requestWithdrawal, changeDisputePeriodSeconds, modifyLocker, modifyFinalizer, changeBlockDurationMillis
Hyperliquid bridge information; confirms native Arbitrum bridge as official channel; describes deposit flow (native USDC to bridge) and withdrawal flow (L1 signature only, 3-4 minute settlement)

unknowns: D2: No canonical 'deployed_contracts_doc' landing page found; bridge addresses documented in API-specific GitBook pages rather than unified deployments reference
D4: Deployment artifacts (Foundry broadcast or hardhat-deploy JSON) not located in GitHub; only source code verified
D5: Hyperliquid L1 chain (not Arbitrum) — no smart contracts discovered; bridge operates one-way from Arbitrum into L1; L1 appears to use Tendermint consensus rather than EVM

protocol_metadata.about: Hyperliquid Bridge is a cross-chain bridge connecting Arbitrum and the Hyperliquid L1 blockchain. Users deposit USDC from Arbitrum to access Hyperliquid's high-performance order-book perpetual DEX. The bridge implements a validator-governed security model with 2/3-quorum approval, dispute periods, and locker-based emergency pause functionality. It is the primary native bridge for assets flowing between Arbitrum and Hyperliquid.
